skill.name: error-resolver
skill.description: Systematic troubleshooting guidance for coding errors, stack traces, runtime failures, dependency issues, configuration problems, and repeatable fix records.
skill.summary: Diagnose software errors with structured classification, root-cause analysis, solution steps, and replay records.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and sensitive-path strings, but repository review shows only Markdown guidance and a YAML replay template. No runnable malicious code or prompt injection was found, but several troubleshooting examples include privileged commands and insecure quick fixes that need user caution.

medium_findings.title: Privileged troubleshooting commands in documentation
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as a user-safety risk, but not as active code execution. The documentation includes examples that start services with sudo, change ownership, add a user to the Docker group, and show broad chmod usage; these can affect host security if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in troubleshooting examples and can change system permissions. Confidence is below very high because they are documented as fixes, not executed by the skill.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive credential paths appear in examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as sensitive-path exposure in guidance, but no exfiltration was found. Git troubleshooting examples reference SSH key files and display a public key workflow, which is common but should be handled carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths and certificate file references are explicit, but the surrounding context is legitimate troubleshooting. No evidence found of network upload or secret collection.

medium_findings.title: Insecure quick fixes are included for debugging
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as risky advice that requires caution. The network guide includes development-only TLS verification disabling, and the Docker guide includes a chmod example marked as not recommended for production.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are real and can weaken security if copied into production. The documentation labels some of them as development-only or not recommended, which reduces risk.

low_findings.title: External command detections are mostly documentation examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malware or hidden execution. The 1076 external command hits are command examples inside Markdown troubleshooting guides, not executable scripts or install hooks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The repository file list contains only Markdown and YAML files, and sampled command hits are fenced examples. No evidence found of executable wrappers invoking these commands.

low_findings.title: Dynamic import and require detections are framework examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for dangerous dynamic loading. The flagged import and require strings are React and Node.js troubleshooting examples, not runtime code shipped by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are in Markdown code fences that explain common framework fixes. No evidence found of a loader that evaluates user-controlled module names.

low_findings.title: Prompt injection indicators were not confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for LLM role-token injection. Targeted review found no text asking the model to ignore instructions, override review, impersonate a system role, or skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static hits appear to come from unrelated command examples near privileged shell text. A repository search found no prompt-injection phrases from the review warning list.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged command examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation includes sudo, service management, ownership changes, and Docker group changes. These are legitimate troubleshooting examples but should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands exist in documentation and can affect host permissions. They are not executed automatically by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad permission workaround
dangerous_patterns.description: The Docker troubleshooting guide includes a broad chmod workaround and marks it as not recommended for production. This is risky if copied blindly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern is explicit and security-relevant. The surrounding warnings lower the likelihood of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: TLS verification disabling example
dangerous_patterns.description: The network guide includes a development-only TLS verification bypass. This can hide certificate problems and should not be used in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The insecure setting is present and clearly security-sensitive. The documentation labels it for development only.

content.value_statement: Debugging often stalls when errors lack clear structure or context. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through diagnosis, root cause, fixes, and reusable records.
actual_capabilities: Classifies errors by category, severity, timing, and likely source.
Extracts useful details from stack traces, messages, file paths, and line numbers.
Matches common Node.js, Python, React, Docker, Git, database, network, Go, and Rust errors.
Uses a five-step workflow for root-cause analysis and solution planning.
Provides templates for recording resolved errors for later reuse.
Suggests verification steps so users can confirm that a fix worked.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not run diagnostics by itself.
Some command examples require user review before execution.
Recorded replay solutions depend on the user maintaining local files.
It cannot guarantee a fix without project context and exact error output.

use_cases.title: Triage a production stack trace
use_cases.description: Classify a runtime failure, identify the likely failing layer, and plan a verified fix.

use_cases.title: Debug local setup failures
use_cases.description: Resolve missing dependencies, permission issues, environment problems, and service connection errors.

use_cases.title: Build a repeatable error knowledge base
use_cases.description: Record recurring failures, known causes, fixes, and verification steps for future incidents.

prompt_templates.title: Explain this error
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-resolver workflow on this error. Classify it, explain the likely cause, and give safe next steps: [paste error].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a stack trace
prompt_templates.prompt: Parse this stack trace. Identify the first useful application frame, root cause candidates, and verification steps: [paste stack trace].

prompt_templates.title: Compare likely fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: For this error and context, compare quick fixes and proper fixes. Highlight commands that need caution: [error and context].

prompt_templates.title: Create a replay record
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this resolved incident into a replay record summary with signature, root cause, solution, prevention, and verification: [details].

output_examples.input: A Node.js app fails with a missing module error after cloning a repository.
output: Classifies the issue as a dependency error.
Explains that dependencies may not be installed or listed correctly.
Suggests checking the package manifest, reinstalling dependencies, and rerunning the app.

output_examples.input: A Docker container cannot write to a mounted directory.
output: Classifies the issue as a permission error.
Compares a quick permission workaround with safer ownership and volume options.
Adds verification steps to confirm the container can write expected files.

output_examples.input: A React page throws a hydration mismatch during rendering.
output: Identifies likely server and client rendering differences.
Suggests isolating browser-only code and checking generated markup.
Recommends prevention steps for deterministic initial rendering.

best_practices: Provide the full error message, stack trace, recent changes, and relevant environment details.
Ask for root cause, immediate fix, proper fix, verification, and prevention in one pass.
Review every suggested command before running it, especially privileged or destructive commands.
anti_patterns: Do not paste only the final error line when a full stack trace is available.
Do not run privileged commands from examples without understanding the impact.
Do not record secrets, tokens, passwords, or private keys in replay solution files.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation that guides diagnosis and suggests steps for the user to review.

faq.question: Can it help with non-code errors?
faq.answer: It is best for software, runtime, build, dependency, network, database, Docker, and Git errors.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the full error, stack trace, recent changes, relevant code, operating system, and tool versions.

faq.question: What is the replay system?
faq.answer: It is a local template for recording resolved errors so similar problems can be solved faster later.

faq.question: Does it replace security review?
faq.answer: No. Commands that change permissions, services, certificates, or credentials still need human review.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
